title: Cornish saffron cake
subtitle: The golden yeasted loaf from the tin-mining parishes of West Cornwall
excerpt: A richly coloured yeasted loaf made with saffron-steeped milk, currants, mixed peel, and lard. Baked in a round tin and sliced thickly, it is the oldest surviving regional bread of Cornwall.
sourceNotes: Cornish saffron cake is documented in Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), Project Gutenberg, which records the lard-and-saffron loaf as a West Cornwall specialty tied to the tin-mining parishes. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes a saffron bread recipe consistent with the regional tradition. The use of saffron in Cornwall is traced to medieval Phoenician spice trade routes through the port of St Ives.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze on the day it is made. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape, place in the tin, cover, and refrigerate overnight. Bring to room temperature for 90 minutes before baking.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dough made richer with eggs, butter, or milk, which slows fermentation and produces a tender, soft crumb.

content.text: Despite the name, Cornish saffron cake is a yeasted loaf rather than a cake. It is a dense, close-crumbed

made with lard, a small amount of butter, currants, mixed peel, and a generous quantity of saffron dissolved in warm milk. The loaf is baked in a round tin, sliced thickly, and eaten buttered. The saffron gives it an unmistakable golden colour and a faint floral bitterness that no other spice can substitute.

Saffron came to West Cornwall through the medieval spice trade. The tin-mining parishes of the Penwith peninsula still bake it today. Bought versions tend to be pale and barely saffron-scented; a properly made home loaf is a different thing entirely. This recipe uses 0.5 g of saffron, which is about one small packet.

attrs.prepNote: steeped in warm milk overnight or for at least 30 minutes

content.text: Infuse the saffron and mix the dough

Steep {{saffron}} in {{whole-milk}} for at least 30 minutes; overnight in the fridge gives a deeper colour. Warm the steeped milk to about 35°C and dissolve {{yeast-dried}} in it. Rub {{lard}} and {{unsalted-butter}} into {{plain-flour}} with {{caster-sugar}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Pour in the saffron milk and mix to a firm dough.

Knead for eight minutes by hand until the dough is smooth and slightly elastic; it is firmer than most bread doughs. Press in the {{currants}}, folding the dough over itself several times to distribute them evenly.

Shape into a ball, cover, and leave in a warm place for about 75 minutes. The dough rises more slowly than a plainer loaf because of the fat and fruit. It should increase by about half again; a full double is not expected.

Grease a 20 cm round cake tin. Shape the dough into a round and press it into the tin. Cover and prove for 30 minutes; the dough will puff but not dramatically. Heat the oven to 180°C fan (200°C conventional). Bake for 40–45 minutes until deep gold and firm. A skewer inserted into the centre should come out clean. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then transfer to a rack. Slice when fully cold.
Lard is traditional and gives the characteristic dense, short crumb. All butter works but the texture is slightly different: softer, less close-grained.
The loaf is best eaten the following day, sliced thick and buttered. The flavour of the saffron intensifies as the loaf rests.
Traditional versions add candied mixed peel alongside the currants; stir 50 g of finely chopped peel into the dough with the currants if you want the older flavour profile.
